sentences.text: A selection, when my mother was selected to the wrong side.
So about 20 or 30 people were put to the wrong side, and they were standing there naked.
And we-- we were all naked, and we were still standing there, and they were marched out to a different direction from where we were going.
And I thought, well, I've lost my mother.
And when I ran to my mother, my mother still whispered to me, go and tell Mini.
Now, we were moved at night to a different part of the camp.
Like I said, you know, they were all small areas, always separate barbed wire, but with a gate on the outside.
And at that time, we were in sort of a double camp.
There was one camp, A camp and B camp, and that was separated by gates with barbed wired and sentries.
And this had happened in A camp, and we were marched in the night to B camp.
And I know Mini was in A Camp.
So-- and I thought, well, I have to go and tell Mini.
And so in the middle of the night, I got out.
Got up from my barrack, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thought I have to go through this gate and to this-- to this other camp.

I remember a lot, but I don't remember how I did it, because I must've done it in a-- in a super-- in a-- in a state completely not human anymore, because it was definitely-- you know, if something would have gone wrong, I would have been shot on the spot.
But I didn't think how I was doing.
I was just doing it.
So somehow, I did get to this camp with searchlight, with soldiers.
I don't know.
And I knew where Mini was, and she was sleeping in her barrack.
And I woke her, and I said to her, Mother's been selected by Mengele-- and see what you can do.
And I left and rushed back.
And the next morning, we were moved again to a different part, and that was all I knew.
Life went on in the camp.
It became winterly weather.
It was cold.
We had terrible clothes.
I became weak.
I became very upset.
I didn't enjoy it anymore at all.
There was no adventure anymore.
I was really scared how will I survive.
I was put into a commando to work on plait-- it was in the barrack.
We had to plait about 30 meters of ropes.
There was on the table to sew rags, but some were very tough.
Some were silk.
Some was some rubbery things.
Some was hard linen.
We had to tear it in bits of strips and then plait it into plaits about an inch wide.
And-- and it had to be very solid.
It was being tested, because it was all different bits so it could be slipping out and it would break.
And they said it was for [? growing ?] hand grenades.
Later it had to be cut in parts of a meter.
And every evenings, we had to do about 300-- 30, I think. 30 meters of these plaits.
And it was counted each evening and tested if it was strong enough.
And if people hadn't done their amounts, they were taken out and gassed.
So to cut already-- well, cut.
We didn't have scissors.
We had to tear it, and with our teeth sometimes to get-- use up the material.
It was a real, very hard work, and we had to really work very, very hard to make our-- our amount.
And we didn't speak.
We didn't-- and it-- it got very, very cold.
We had just the-- the ground which got frozen, and we had more-- we had shoes, but no socks or anything.
I got terrible frostbite I got open wounds on my toes.
And I say, we just sat there over 10, 12 hours plaiting.
And our hands got swollen, and-- and some-- but it-- it was so cold, we could hardly feel anything.
That was really terrible work.
And I say, we had to be quick.
We couldn't let up at all.
And one day I was sitting there, and a couple came in and said, you're wanted outside.
And I thought, oh, gosh.
That must be a Nazi who wants me for something.
And I was very, very scared, and I very reluctantly went outside.
And my father stood there.
And again, miracle.
That in this camp where I didn't even know in which part I was, and-- I don't know.
And I never asked him how he was able to find me, but he was there, and he looked quite well.
And he said, I came with my boss.
They were still working in the timber factory.
And asked him how Heinz was and he said he's all right.
And I said, you really-- are you sure?
Yeah, yeah, definitely.
He's OK.
And then he asked me where my mother was.
And I started to cry, and I said she's-- she's dead.
She's been selected by Mengele.
And, of course, that was a terrible blow for my father.

And I always feel a bit guilty about this, because I think if he would have known in the end after Heinz died, if he would have thought perhaps that my mother and perhaps I was still alive, he might have carried on.
He might just have survived.
Because he died just two or three days before the liberation.
But thinking that he would have been the only survivor, I think he just gave up.
But I didn't know any better, and, you know, I just felt I had to tell him the truth, which I thought was the truth.
And, of course, he-- I saw he was very upset, and he just said, well, try to be strong, and, you know, we'll come [? through ?] and we'll make a life again.
And he knew that the invasion had been, and he knew the Russians [BACKROUND NOISE] were near.
It was already towards the end of the war.
We did know that it can't go on anymore for years.
So he tried to give me hope, and he said, in the kitchen, I've got a friend.
He was a very charming man, and he must have made friends with somebody in the kitchen.
And when you go there in the evening, she'll give you some extra food.
Which I did, and there was a-- was a Polish woman.
And she said, your father is marvelous.
And she always had some scraps of food for me.
So, you know, it was really amazing.
And he-- he came another time, and that was really then-- twice he came, and that was the last day I've seen him.
But I never asked him how he managed to find me or how he came there.
So I struggled on, and it got colder and it was more difficult.
And we did hear guns, so that we noticed the Germans became rather nervous and they started to evacuate the camps.
Every day, the Nazis came in and picked people [POINTING] from our commando-- out, out, out.
And those people were sent on those terrible march commandos, death marches, where a lot of people died.
And one day, they came and wanted to pick me out.
And then the kapo said, no, not her.
She's a protection of the Germans.
So again, she must have known that there was some connection.
Perhaps she saw that if my mother come and I'm not here, then there might be trouble.
I mean, I don't know.
But anyway, so they left me alone there.
And again, that was something which saved me, because not many people survived those terrible marches.
They walked for three weeks into German concentration camps through midwinter.
And if people rested a minute or sat down, they were shot, so again, I was protected and saved.
I had one friend.
She was really called Nini, but in my book I call her [?
Francie, ?] and she was from our Dutch transport.
And she was with me most of us the time that I wasn't with my mother.
We slept together, and we worked together.
But one day, she was taken away on those marched transports, and she actually did survive.
She got TB, and she was for many, many years afterwards in Holland, she was bedridden.
But after a year, she recovered and she's still alive.
She immigrated to Israel.
But so then I was really left all on my own.
There was nobody anymore from our original Dutch transport.

And one day, I was walking around aimlessly, very depressed and very sore feet and really more or less at the end of my strength, and a Dutch woman who had come from our transport walked across, and who I hadn't seen.
And she said, I just came from another part [POINTING] of the camp from the hospital.
At that time, they had the whole-- whole camp with hospital barracks.
It was one of the experiment blocks were Mengele worked.
First it was always at the end of a-- of a camp was one barrack, but then they had the whole part of it.
And [INAUDIBLE] have just come from there, and I've seen your mother.
And I said, I don't believe it.
You know, I thought she just wanted to give me some hope.
And so she said, yes, yes.
She's quite well, and she's in hospital.
And Mini's there as-- as a nurse, and Mini protects her.
And, you know, she's all right.
So, of course, that was wonderful for me.
And you could go if you reported-- said you were ill, you could go for the day to this hospital block for treatment.
And she said, put-- she said, put your name down that you-- I'm sure you have some illness.
I said yes, I've got terrible frostbite.
She said, oh, wonderful.
Put that down, and then you'll be able to go to her.
And I had to wait some time, but eventually I was called out for to go to the hospital department.
And she told me this block and this and this, you should try to get there.
And again, I [? did that ?].
I mean, everything was really difficult and impossible, but somehow we got-- we were able to do those things which we're not allowed to.
And I went to this hospital where the treatment was, but I ran off and I went to this block where I knew Mini was and my mother.
And that time at the last shower, which we got, I got a huge, very, very heavy men's coat.
That was all I had.
That was my clothes.
But it was at least warm, but I could hardly carry myself with it.

And it was very, very cold, and I got into this block where Mini was and they were on-- at the end of the block where they worked at a an office, but there was a little hole, a little space where they had some medicine and some paperwork and things.
And I said to-- I went to her and said, Mini.
She didn't recognize me at that time.
And then I said, I'm [?
Evi. ?] Oh, ha, ha, ha.
She said, oh, I must go and-- come, come.
Must tell your mother.
And I was very red because of from the cold, and with this big coat I looked quite healthy and-- and strong.
And she took me there, and in the hospital blocks there were just bunk beds-- two, not those with 10 people.
Just two on top of each other, but two people slept in each one.
So and my mother was on the top bunk quite weak and not very well, and my mother-- then Fritzi, I've got somebody here to show you.
And my mother crawled down and saw me in this enormous coat and red, and she-- she kind of came down very weak, you know?
It was difficult.
And we embraced and we cried, and she wanted to see how I looked.
And she opened that coat, and I was a sort of a skeleton.
And she looked terrible too, you know.
It was-- it was-- it was wonderful, but it was terrible.
And she could hardly stand and very, very weak.
And then Mini said, you must report that you can't work anymore, and I will do some work.
And you have to go back tonight, but I will see that when you come again that you can stay here.
And somehow, we managed that.
And by that time-- it must have been December-- and there were very few people left who were still working, because the camp was practically empty at that time already.
And-- and that's why one could do things like that.
You know, could go to the hospital, I think.
They were not anymore so strict.
A lot of the SS had gone away anyway as well, and escaped back to Germany.
And the crematorium had-- they were not working anymore.
So things were in a way more relaxed, but on the other hand, more disorganized.
The food was very irregular.
Sometimes you got something.
Sometimes you didn't.
But anyway, I did organize that that I would be able to-- oh, there's still one other incident I would like to tell.
When we were still in this bunk bed, still in this where I worked, one night I felt something going over me, and I thought it was somebody who wanted to steal my bread.
And I kind of moved, and then I realized it was a rat which had kind of walked over me and was trying to nibble on my toes, which were bloody.
So you can imagine what a feeling that was when I realized that a huge rat was kind of trying to eat my feet.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and another terrible incident was-- that was still when there were more people at night-- we had at the end of each barrack in winter, they put a bucket so that we didn't have to go to the toilet so far to this one block where there was a toilet.
And-- but if it was full, we had to empty it.
The one who was last and it was full had to take this bucket in the night to this place and empty it.
And that was, of course, terrible job.
First of all, people left it so full that when you did carry it, it all spilled over.
And it took a long time.
And so everybody tried to avoid that.
And so once somebody had emptied it-- instead of taking it, they had emptied it outside just in the back of the barrack.
And the kapo had seen that in the morning.
There was, again, a full bucket, but they'd seen the mess which was outside.
So she came in, called us out all-- we had to get up.
And we all stood in front of our beds, and she shouted and said, again, you dirty Jews and [GERMAN].
That was an expression and [GERMAN] and, you know, terrible swearing they did.
And, look what you've done.
You can't even carry your own shit and blah, blah blah.
And she called somebody and gave her a muck and said, throw this all on you.
Then you will see how you behave.
And we had to empty all this stuff on-- throw it on all the inmates.
I mean, you know, terrible.
And we had no way of washing, so we were like this going around for days.
No wonder the rats came, and it was terrible.
So-- but by that time, then I-- were only a few people, so this didn't happen so much then because there was not so much buckets, things to carry away.
So I organized.
I said I would go again to this hospital department camp.
And when I got there, Mini talk-- had talked to the man in charge, the doctor in charge there, and they said, yes, your frostbite is really-- you can't really work.
You can stay in the hospital.
At that time as I said, there were no selections, so it was in a way safe to stay in the hospital.
But before, it wasn't, and most of the people who had been before in the hospital were selected out.
But Mini had protected my mother.
That's-- that's how she did stay in.
But because she didn't work and she didn't get out really, that's why she was really very, very weak.
And so we stayed like that for a few weeks, and there were very few inmates and very few Germans.
It was really more or less a deserted camp.
It was mid winter.
It was minus 30 degrees.
[POINTING] There was everything covered in snow.
It was-- we got practically no food.
There was nothing coming anymore.
And then one day, the Germans said, we are going to evacuate the whole camp now, and we're going to march in the morning.
So everybody get ready.
Well, most people in the hospital block were really not able-- they were not even able to stand or not, so they couldn't.
But everybody who just could stand and walk intended to go, because what we assumed was that when they leave, they will close up the barracks and burn it.
Later we heard that those were indeed the orders to the Wehrmacht.
They were supposed to destroy everything.
But the Russians were approaching very fast, and we heard every day guns and airplanes.
And when they had [POINTING] alarms going for-- when the planes came, they sounded the alarm.
And we were not marching, because there were quite a few Germans as well with us still.
So when the alarm went they said, go in till it's over and then we'll call you out again.
So that went on for several days.
We stood ready to march, even sometimes started to march then alarm went again, and in we went again.
And my mother as well.
We were all very exhausted.
A few hundred people, perhaps.
And in the evening, again, they called out march, and we went back in again.
And I said, I'm not going out anymore.
If they call again, I'll stay.
Don't go anyway.
So we slept through the night.
We were so tired.
And in the morning, we woke up and they were gone.
Mini as well.
She was still on this transport as well.
She was still [? the ?] [? end, ?] and she had said to us, you must go, because you won't come out alive here.
So but anyways, they were gone, and we are still alive.
And there were no Germans.
So we were all on our own with perhaps 50, 60 other people, but all-- just a handful who could really walk around.
And so myself and a few other people walked around, because now we could walk freely in the camp, and we searched and we looked.
There was really nothing at all there.

And then we somehow we-- we saw that the barbed-- the electricity wasn't working, so we crawled very-- again, it was-- if it would have been working, we'd have been-- but one of us crawled through the barbed wire to another camp.
Because we were going to look if there were other people that perhaps feed us and ask if we could do something.
And so we-- it wasn't switched on anymore, the electricity, and we went through several camps.
But there was nobody around.
And then we came to one whole camp, which was where the food was stored.
And that was a [INAUDIBLE] [? cave ?] in food.
There was everything there.
And what we were mainly interested was bread, because that we could eat.
There was no-- no cooking facility or anything, of course.
But things which we could eat, just grab like that and eat, we took.
So the few of us carried bread back to the people who couldn't walk around, and we gave this to them.
And then there was the problem of water.
Everything was frozen.
In the taps there was no water at all.
So in the entrance of the camp where the Germans had lived-- because each camp had little barracks where the guards were living-- there was a little pond.
And that was, of course, frozen, but we found some instrument and we hacked through the ice.
And we found then as well clothes and blankets and tools.
We found everything.
And I took as well some [? eiderdowns, ?] which I still have, which, you know, which came from transport.
It was stored.
And I took some big leather boots, which I had for a long time till Odessa when I swapped it for a chicken.
And anyway, we got water from this pond, and we-- it was icy cold.
But then later, we found in this-- those barracks where the SS lived, there was a stove and wood, and we could cook.
And then my-- my mother and a Polish woman and a French girl decided we would leave the camp and live in this barrack, the four of us, because it was terrible in that hospital block.
Another thing which still happened.
There were still a few people who could walk, and most people were dying every day.
And one day an older woman called me and she said, come here.
We need you to carry a body out.
And I said, no, no.
I can't do that.
I can't do that.
And so my mother was still in her bed, and she said, I'll come down and carry that.
But she didn't have the strength to carry, so we together carried.
And there was outside the blocks, it was-- everything was frozen.
We just heaped the bodies up, and it was, you know, it was-- it was terrible.
All stiff and frozen and-- But it was beautiful weather.
It was everything was in snow and blue sky.
And the sun shone on this-- you know, it-- it just didn't fit together at all, this image.
But I will still remember this beautiful, sparkling, diamond snow and just all those bodies heaped in a corner.
But my mother decided that, you know, there's no point staying there, and we will move into this-- into those barracks where the Nazis were staying.
So we were there, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we spent a few days like that, not really knowing what to do and just hoping the Germans wouldn't come back and the Russians weren't coming.
And it was good, but it was terrible as well.
And one day, my mother was hacking ice in the pond, and a lorry came.
And me and the other people were in the house, because we didn't go out when it wasn't necessary.
It was terribly cold.
And we looked, and we saw it was Germans.
And they said something to my mother, and I saw that she went with them, walked with them, and then they went into the camp.
And they seemed to round up some more people who were still alive there, and who they tried to get out to walk.
And they were coming back to take those people to walk still-- make more transport.
And we watched petrified that they would come in and-- but we hid there, and they never came and looked in the house.
But my mother, we-- we-- we saw then that she walked with them and went out of the camp.
And a few hours later, she came back.
It got dark then, and when they couldn't see so well, the Germans, she dropped dead-- she dropped down and pretended to be dead.
And they left-- let her lie there and they walked away.
And then she came back, so that was another moment where she-- where I thought, again, I had lost her.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we still said it's too dangerous to-- we didn't we didn't know what to do.
But then one morning we were in the camp looking, again, for more clothes and more things, and we heard, again, movement and things.
And we saw at the camp-- we saw-- I saw a huge figure standing and all covered in snow and fur.
And the first minute, I thought it was a bear, and I wasn't quite sure, should I go there or what.
And then I went a bit closer and I looked, and I saw that it was a man.
I saw it was the first Russian soldier who had entered the camp.
